quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Position, velocity, acceleration"?

options.text: Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C
s(t) → v(t) = s'(t) → a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t); each is the derivative of the previous
Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape

quiz_questions.explanation: "Position, velocity, acceleration" is defined as: s(t) → v(t) = s'(t) → a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t); each is the derivative of the previous. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Speed vs. velocity"?

options.text: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
State the theorem precisely, verify hypotheses, apply the conclusion, and interpret in the context of the problem
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
Speed = |v(t)| (always non-negative); velocity v(t) includes direction (sign): positive = moving right/up, negative = moving left/down

quiz_questions.explanation: "Speed vs. velocity" is defined as: Speed = |v(t)| (always non-negative); velocity v(t) includes direction (sign): positive = moving right/up, negative = moving left/down. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Speeding up vs. slowing down"?

options.text: Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)
Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs

quiz_questions.explanation: "Speeding up vs. slowing down" is defined as: Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Displacement vs. distance"?

options.text: A constant solution y = c where f(x,c) = 0 for all x; appears as horizontal segments in the slope field
Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
Displacement = ∫ₐᵇ v(t)dt = s(b) - s(a) (net change); distance = ∫ₐᵇ |v(t)|dt (total path length)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Displacement vs. distance" is defined as: Displacement = ∫ₐᵇ v(t)dt = s(b) - s(a) (net change); distance = ∫ₐᵇ |v(t)|dt (total path length). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Change of direction"?

options.text: A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
G = 2∫₀¹ [x - L(x)]dx; ranges from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (perfect inequality); measures income distribution
An object changes direction when v(t) = 0 and v changes sign; these points divide the time interval for distance calculations
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape

quiz_questions.explanation: "Change of direction" is defined as: An object changes direction when v(t) = 0 and v changes sign; these points divide the time interval for distance calculations. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "s(t) → v(t) = s'(t) → a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t); each is the derivative of the previous"?

options.text: Conservative forces
Comparison with first derivative test
Position, velocity, acceleration
Limit does not exist

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Position, velocity, acceleration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Speed = |v(t)| (always non-negative); velocity v(t) includes direction (sign): positive = moving right/up, negative = moving left/down"?

options.text: Speed vs. velocity
Acceleration
Integration by parts indicators
d/dx[sin x] = cos x

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Speed vs. velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Speeding up: v(t) and a(t) have the same sign; slowing down: they have opposite signs"?

options.text: Hypothesis checking
Fractional exponent derivatives
Angular rate problems
Speeding up vs. slowing down

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Speeding up vs. slowing down".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Displacement = ∫ₐᵇ v(t)dt = s(b) - s(a) (net change); distance = ∫ₐᵇ |v(t)|dt (total path length)"?

options.text: Displacement vs. distance
Derivative of tan x
Logistic DE
Combining with u-substitution

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Displacement vs. distance".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An object changes direction when v(t) = 0 and v changes sign; these points divide the time interval for distance calculations"?

options.text: Change of direction
Fractional exponent derivatives
d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]
Cardiac output

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Change of direction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Motion Problems is accurate?

options.text: Motion Problems apply derivatives and integrals to analyze the motion of objects along a line.
Introduction to Integrals presents integration as the reverse of differentiation and as the accumulation of quantities.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.
The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Motion Problems as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Motion Problems?

options.text: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
An infinite limit lim(x→c) f(x) = ±∞ means f(x) increases or decreases without bound as x approaches c.
Position s(t), velocity v(t) = s'(t), and acceleration a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) form the derivative chain.
Graphs also reveal asymptotic behavior and discontinuities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Motion Problems as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Motion Problems?

options.text: Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.
These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.
An object is speeding up when v and a have the same sign, slowing down when they have opposite signs.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Motion Problems as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Motion Problems?

options.text: These formulas arise from the Pythagorean theorem applied to infinitesimal arc segments: ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²)dx.
Total distance traveled is ∫|v(t)|dt, which differs from displacement ∫v(t)dt = s(b) - s(a) when the object changes direction.
The key insight is that pressure increases with depth, so different parts of the plate experience different pressures.
A slope field (direction field) displays short line segments at grid points (x,y), each with slope equal to f(x,y) from dy/dx = f(x,y).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Motion Problems as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Motion Problems?

options.text: Average Value of a Function extends the concept of arithmetic mean to continuous functions.
Motion Problems apply derivatives and integrals to analyze the motion of objects along a line.
The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.
Arc Length and Surface Area uses integration to measure curve length and rotational surface area.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Motion Problems as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Motion Problems?

options.text: Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Analyzing car acceleration: v(t) = 60(1-e^(-0.5t)) mph models a car from rest; s'(t) gives instantaneous speed and s''(t) shows the acceleration decreasing over time
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Motion Problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Motion Problems?

options.text: Newton's method in GPS: iteratively solving nonlinear equations to determine position from satellite distance measurements
Elevator motion control: position, velocity, and acceleration constraints ensure smooth rides—jerk (a'(t)) must be limited for passenger comfort
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Motion Problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Motion Problems applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Moore's Law: the roughly exponential growth of transistor density in microchips (doubling every ~2 years) eventually creating unforeseen limits in physics
Projectile motion: h(t) = -16t² + v₀t + h₀ gives height, h'(t) = -32t + v₀ gives velocity, and h''(t) = -32 ft/s² is the constant gravitational acceleration
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Motion Problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Motion Problems?

options.text: Analyzing car acceleration: v(t) = 60(1-e^(-0.5t)) mph models a car from rest; s'(t) gives instantaneous speed and s''(t) shows the acceleration decreasing over time
AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning
Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Motion Problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Motion Problems in practice?

options.text: Navigation: calculating bearing angles using arctan(Δy/Δx) and differentiating to find rate of angle change for tracking systems
Elevator motion control: position, velocity, and acceleration constraints ensure smooth rides—jerk (a'(t)) must be limited for passenger comfort
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Surveillance camera tracking: as a car drives past a fixed camera, the rate of angle change dθ/dt determines how fast the camera must pan to keep the car in frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Motion Problems.
